metadata.title: Why Claude Can't Write Your Literature Review
metadata.purpose: Claude generates text with the statistical pattern of citations — author, journal, year, finding — using the same mechanism for real and hallucinated entries. Citations from Claude are leads for verification, not evidence; treating them as evidence is the most common research error.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: INK = real/verified sources (green-light); CRIMSON = hallucinated citations (identical surface, no source); SLATE = plausible-but-unverified
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO semantic entropy or calibration formalism, NO retrieval-augmented generation as solution, NO comparison of citation accuracy across model versions. Mechanism only: same statistical pattern generates real and hallucinated entries; surface format gives no signal.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Claude returned twelve citations. Author, journal, year, page range — each one perfectly formatted. Four linked to real papers. Three had real journals but wrong findings. Five did not exist at all. All twelve looked identical.

card.copy: Why Claude can't write your literature review
card.sub: citations from Claude are leads — not evidence

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is Liam, in for Bear. Claude returned twelve citations. Author, journal, year, page range — each one perfectly formatted. Four linked to real papers. Three

beats.narration_text: Marco is a PhD student. He asks Claude for five papers on green roof cooling effects. He gets five citations with names, journals, years, and findings. Two exist and say what Claude claims. One exists but says the opposite. Two have real author names — but the papers don't exist. All five look identical when he receives them.
beats.scene_description: Graduate student at laptop cross-checking citations one by one against a database, looking increasingly concerned, editorial newsprint treatment
beats.image_prompt: B02, graduate student man at laptop looking concerned while cross-checking research citations against a database on a second screen, editorial documentary newsprint treatment, desaturated flat print collage, university library environment

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Marco is a PhD student. He asks Claude for five papers on green roof cooling effects. He gets five citations with names, journals, years, and findings. Two exis

beats.narration_text: Marco received twelve citations that look identical in format. Four were real. Eight weren't. Why did the format give no signal about which were which?

card.copy: Why did the format give no signal?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Marco received twelve citations that look identical in format. Four were real. Eight weren't. Why did the format give no signal about which were which?

beats.narration_text: Claude does not retrieve sources from a database. It generates text that has the statistical shape of scholarly writing — including citations. A citation is a hypothesis about what a relevant source might look like. Some hypotheses correspond to real publications. Some do not.

The hallucinated citation uses the same pattern as the real one. Author. Journal. Year. Volume. Pages. Finding. The model generates all six fields using the same statistical process — whether the source exists or not. There is no internal flag that marks one as real.

The voice of scholarship does not prove the substance of scholarship. Fluency is not accuracy. A citation that sounds authoritative — precise year, volume, page range, confident claim — may have been generated on the same basis as the five words before and after it.

The distinction that protects you: leads versus evidence. A lead is a direction worth investigating. Evidence is a verified source that says what you claim it says. Claude can only provide leads. Leads require verification before they become evidence.

card.copy: Claude provides leads. You produce the evidence.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The distinction that protects you: leads versus evidence. A lead is a direction worth investigating. Evidence is a verified source that says what you claim it s

beats.narration_text: The prompt that works: ask Claude for search terms and framework names — not citations. 'Give me eight search terms for this topic. Name the relevant theoretical frameworks — no citations, I will find the primary sources.' That is a leads-first prompt. The leads are cheap and useful. The verification step is yours.

This applies to any Claude-assisted research task. The richer the output looks — the more complete, the more authoritative — the more important it is to verify each claim independently. Fluency is the signal that makes the check feel unnecessary. That is precisely when it is most necessary.

Marco's twelve citations: he checks each one against the database. Four resolve to real papers that match the claim. Three resolve to real journals with different or reversed findings. Five return no result at all — the papers do not exist. All twelve citations had the same format. Only four were evidence.

With a leads-first prompt: Marco asks Claude for search terms and framework names only — no citations. He runs the terms in Google Scholar and PubMed. He finds eleven real papers in forty minutes. He provides three abstracts to Claude and asks for a literature matrix from those sources only. Claude works only from what he provided.

The practice: two rules for every research prompt. First — never ask Claude for citations directly; ask for search terms and framework names. Second — if you paste sources to Claude, ask it to work only from what you provided and flag anything it cannot find in your text.
A citation from Claude is a hypothesis — not a source. Ask for leads. Verify the leads before they become evidence. The voice of scholarship does not prove the substance of scholarship.

card.copy: Leads to verify. Not evidence to cite.
card.sub: The voice of scholarship does not prove the substance.
